en.extract:
The brown-backed honeyeater (Ramsayornis modestus) is a species of bird in the family Meliphagidae. It is found in New Guinea and Cape York Peninsula in Queensland, Australia. Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical mangrove forests.


Description
The Brown-backed honeyeater is small in size, measuring around 11–12cm in length with a weight of 11–14 g. The average wingspan is 6.2–7 cm (2.4 to 2.7 in), and the bill is 1.5–1.8 cm in length. Like the name suggests, it has a brown back with brown streaks across an otherwise white chest and belly, and a red bill. Similar in appearance to the Bar-breasted Honeyeater, but with a differing facial pattern.


Calls
Calls include a rapid mick, mick, and a chattering shee-shee.


Distribution
The Brown-backed honeyeater is found along the coast of Far North Queensland, particularly around the Cape York Peninsula. Its range extends across the islands of the Torres Strait and into New Guinea.
Unlike similar birds that migrate across the Torres Strait, the Brown-backed honeyeater is partly resident and nomadic, meaning it tends to stay in the same area year-round but will travel depending on environmental factors, such as food availability and rainfall. It is thought to be seasonally migratory in parts of north east Queensland, moving south in August and north during the spring.


Habitat
It is found in a wide variety of tropical or subtropical habitats near water in coastal areas, including open grassy woodlands, swamps, rainforests, mangroves, and gardens. Normally found individually or in pairs.


Feeding
The Brown-backed honeyeater feeds on small insects found in foliage and in flight, and nectar when available.


References

The '''brown-backed honeyeater''' ('''''Ramsayornis modestus''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Meliphagidae]]. It is found in [[New Guinea]] and [[Cape York Peninsula]] in [[Queensland]], Australia. Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical mangrove [[forest]]s.

== Description ==
The Brown-backed honeyeater is small in size, measuring around 11–12cm in length with a weight of 11–14 g. The average wingspan is 6.2–7 cm (2.4 to 2.7 in), and the bill is 1.5–1.8 cm in length. Like the name suggests, it has a brown back with brown streaks across an otherwise white chest and belly, and a red bill. Similar in appearance to the [[Bar-breasted Honeyeater]], but with a differing facial pattern.<ref name=" abc ">{{Cite book|title=The Australian Bird Guide|last1=Menkhorst |first1=P. |last2=Rogers |first2=D. |last3=Clarke |first3=R. |last4=Davies |first4=J. |last5=Marsack |first5=P. |last6=Franklin |first6=K.|publisher=CSIRO Publications|isbn=978-1-4863-1193-4|pages=390–391|year=2019|edition=Revised |location=Victoria}}</ref><ref name=" ab ">{{cite book |last1=Slater |first1=Peter |last2=Slater |first2=Pat |last3=Slater |first3=Raoul |title=[[The Slater Field Guide to Australian Birds]]|pages=292|year=1995 |edition=Revised |isbn=0-947116-99-0 |publisher=Lansdowne |location=Sydney, Australia}}</ref>

===Calls===
Calls include a rapid ''mick, mick'', and a chattering ''shee-shee''.<ref name=" ab " />

==Distribution==
The Brown-backed honeyeater is found along the coast of [[Far North Queensland]], particularly around the [[Cape York Peninsula]]. Its range extends across the islands of the [[Torres Strait]] and into [[New Guinea]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Brown-backed Honeyeater - eBird |url=https://ebird.org/species/brbhon1?siteLanguage=en_AU|access-date=2025-11-27 |website=ebird.org |language=en}}</ref>

Unlike similar birds that migrate across the Torres Strait, the Brown-backed honeyeater is partly resident and nomadic, meaning it tends to stay in the same area year-round but will travel depending on environmental factors, such as food availability and rainfall. It is thought to be seasonally migratory in parts of north east Queensland, moving south in August and north during the spring.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Higgins|first1= P. J.|last2=Christidis |first2=L.|first3=H.|last3=Ford |editor-last=del Hoyo |editor-first=J. |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-first2=A.|editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-first3=J. |editor-last4=Christie  |editor-first4=D. A. |editor-last5=de Juana|editor-first5= E. |date=2020 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/brbhon1/cur/introduction|title=Brown-backed Honeyeater (Ramsayornis modestus), version 1.0. In Birds of the World|journal=[[Birds of the World]]|location= Ithaca, NY, USA. |doi=10.2173/bow.brbhon1.01|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

==Habitat==
It is found in a wide variety of tropical or subtropical habitats near water in coastal areas, including open grassy woodlands, swamps, rainforests, mangroves, and gardens. Normally found individually or in pairs.<ref name=" abc " />

===Feeding=== 
The Brown-backed honeyeater feeds on small insects found in foliage and in flight, and nectar when available.<ref name=" abc " />
